content.text: GenAI experiments expose users to model outputs that can be factually wrong, biased, or harmful in ways conventional software never produces. A feature flag A/B test either shows a button or doesn't; a GenAI A/B test may expose thousands of users to a model that subtly reinforces stereotypes or produces unsafe advice. Governance gates — structured review checkpoints before, during, and after an experiment — are the mechanism by which teams catch these harms before they reach scale.
Why GenAI Experiments Need Explicit Governance
Conventional software experimentation governance is mostly a product and legal formality. GenAI experiments require genuine ethics infrastructure because:
Output is stochastic and hard to audit exhaustively before launch — the risk surface is sampled, not enumerated.
Harms are asymmetric: a user harmed by bad AI advice is not equivalent to a user who saw the wrong button color.
Subgroup disparities are invisible in aggregate metrics — a model may perform better overall while failing a demographic subgroup.
Novelty effects inflate early metrics, masking long-term quality degradation.
Regulatory exposure (EU AI Act, sector-specific AI rules) attaches to production experiments, not just deployed products.

The purpose of experiment governance is not to block progress. It is to ensure that when something does go wrong — and something will — the team can demonstrate due diligence, stop quickly, and recover completely.

attrs.alt: Experiment governance pipeline with five stages and four review gates

content.text: Triggered when an experiment is submitted to the governance system. An automated classifier scores the proposal on three risk dimensions:

The composite risk score determines the review track. Low-risk experiments proceed through automated Gate 1 in under 24 hours. High-risk experiments go to the full ethics board with a 5-business-day SLA.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional
import uuid

class RiskLevel(Enum):
    LOW = "low"
    MEDIUM = "medium"
    HIGH = "high"

@dataclass
class ExperimentProposal:
    id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    title: str = ""
    hypothesis: str = ""
    traffic_fraction: float = 0.0   # 0.0–1.0
    user_segment: str = ""          # e.g. "all", "premium", "healthcare_users"
    output_domain: str = ""         # e.g. "summarization", "health_advice"
    model_delta: str = ""           # e.g. "prompt_only", "model_swap"
    estimated_sample_size: int = 0
    minimum_runtime_days: int = 14

    def risk_level(self) -> RiskLevel:
        score = 0
        if self.traffic_fraction > 0.10:   score += 2
        elif self.traffic_fraction > 0.01: score += 1
        if self.output_domain in ("health_advice", "financial_advice", "legal_advice"):
            score += 2
        elif self.output_domain in ("recommendations", "routing"):
            score += 1
        if self.model_delta == "model_swap" or "fine_tune" in self.model_delta:
            score += 2
        elif self.model_delta == "prompt_strategy":
            score += 1
        if self.user_segment in ("healthcare_users", "minors", "crisis_users"):
            score += 2
        if score >= 4:   return RiskLevel.HIGH
        if score >= 2:   return RiskLevel.MEDIUM
        return RiskLevel.LOW
Ethics review evaluates three concerns that product teams are not well-positioned to self-assess:

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Harm assessment', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' — structured checklist covering psychological harm, discrimination, misinformation amplification, and dependency risk.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Bias and fairness analysis plan', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' — the reviewer must confirm that subgroup metrics (by age, gender, geography, language) are pre-registered in the experiment plan before launch, not analyzed post-hoc.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Data privacy and consent', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' — are experiment logs tied to identifiable users? Is that data handling covered by the existing privacy policy, or does it require updated notice?'}

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Optional
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class HarmAssessment:
    psychological_harm_risk: str    # "none", "low", "medium", "high"
    discrimination_risk: str
    misinformation_risk: str
    dependency_risk: str
    mitigations: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    residual_risk: str = "unknown"

@dataclass
class FairnessAnalysisPlan:
    preregistered_subgroups: List[str]   # ["age_bracket", "preferred_language"]
    parity_metric: str                   # "demographic_parity" | "equalized_odds"
    disparity_threshold: float           # e.g. 0.05 — halt if > 5% gap

@dataclass
class EthicsReview:
    proposal_id: str
    reviewer_id: str
    review_track: str            # "automated", "expedited", "full_board"
    harm_assessment: HarmAssessment
    fairness_plan: FairnessAnalysisPlan
    privacy_impact: str          # "covered", "needs_notice_update", "blocked"
    decision: Optional[str] = None   # "approved", "approved_with_conditions", "rejected"
    conditions: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    reviewed_at: Optional[datetime] = None

    def approve(self, conditions: List[str] = None):
        self.decision = "approved_with_conditions" if conditions else "approved"
        self.conditions = conditions or []
        self.reviewed_at = datetime.utcnow()

    def reject(self, reason: str):
        self.decision = "rejected"
        self.conditions = [reason]
        self.reviewed_at = datetime.utcnow()

    def is_approved(self) -> bool:
        return self.decision in ("approved", "approved_with_conditions")
Gate 3 is a pre-flight technical check run by the experiment platform, not the ethics board. It confirms the operational prerequisites are in place before traffic splits:

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Guardrail thresholds', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' — harm rate, refusal rate, and subgroup parity thresholds are configured in the monitoring system, not left as "we\'ll check manually".'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Rollback plan', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' — the feature flag can be flipped with one command, and the team has verified this in staging within the past 48 hours.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Stakeholder sign-off', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' — product, legal, and the ethics reviewer record explicit approval with timestamps. This log is immutable.'}
Gate 4 is not a one-time checkpoint — it is an always-on runtime layer that runs from experiment launch until the traffic split closes. Three monitors operate in parallel:
Every hour, the monitor computes the harm rate and subgroup disparity from the past window. If either exceeds the configured threshold, the experiment auto-halts:

import statistics
from typing import List, Dict, Tuple
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class HourlyExperimentSnapshot:
    experiment_id: str
    arm: str                  # "control" | "treatment"
    harm_events: int
    total_exposures: int
    subgroup_metrics: Dict[str, float]  # subgroup -> quality score

def evaluate_guardrails(
    treatment: HourlyExperimentSnapshot,
    control: HourlyExperimentSnapshot,
    config: "GuardrailConfig",
) -> Tuple[bool, str]:
    """Return (should_halt, reason)."""
    if treatment.total_exposures == 0:
        return False, "no_data"

    harm_rate = treatment.harm_events / treatment.total_exposures
    if harm_rate > config.max_harm_rate:
        return True, f"harm_rate={harm_rate:.4f} > threshold={config.max_harm_rate}"

    if treatment.subgroup_metrics and control.subgroup_metrics:
        control_mean = statistics.mean(control.subgroup_metrics.values())
        for subgroup, score in treatment.subgroup_metrics.items():
            control_subgroup = control.subgroup_metrics.get(subgroup, control_mean)
            disparity = abs(score - control_subgroup) / max(control_subgroup, 0.001)
            if disparity > config.max_subgroup_disparity:
                return True, f"subgroup_disparity[{subgroup}]={disparity:.3f}"

    return False, "ok"

def auto_halt_experiment(experiment_id: str, reason: str, flag_service) -> None:
    """Immediately disable the experiment traffic split."""
    flag_service.disable(experiment_id)
    # Structured alert — on-call gets paged within 2 minutes
    print(f"EXPERIMENT_AUTO_HALT experiment={experiment_id} reason={reason}")

Runtime monitoring must not be confused with the statistical analysis that determines the experiment outcome. The monitoring layer looks at safety guardrail metrics only — not the primary success metric. Looking at the primary metric before the pre-registered sample size is reached ("peeking") inflates false positive rates:
After the experiment concludes — either by reaching the target sample size or being stopped — a mandatory post-experiment review documents what happened and certifies the ship decision:
Were subgroup metrics analyzed for all pre-registered groups?
Did any guardrail breach occur? What caused it?
Was the no-peeking rule maintained throughout?
Does shipping align with the approved ethics conditions?
Documented decision with rationale, stored in experiment registry

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Optional
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class PostExperimentReview:
    experiment_id: str
    subgroup_results: dict            # subgroup -> {metric: value}
    guardrail_breaches: List[dict]    # list of auto-halt events, empty if none
    no_peeking_violations: int        # times primary metric was accessed early
    ethics_conditions_met: bool
    ship_decision: Optional[str] = None   # "ship", "no_ship", "ship_with_guardrails"
    decision_rationale: str = ""
    decided_at: Optional[datetime] = None

    def record_decision(self, decision: str, rationale: str):
        assert decision in ("ship", "no_ship", "ship_with_guardrails")
        self.ship_decision = decision
        self.decision_rationale = rationale
        self.decided_at = datetime.utcnow()

    def is_reviewable(self) -> bool:
        """All pre-conditions for a valid review must hold."""
        return (
            len(self.subgroup_results) > 0
            and self.no_peeking_violations == 0
        )
Governance works only when accountability is specific — not "the team" but named roles:
Submit proposal with accurate risk classification; hold rollback authority
Harm and fairness assessment; post-experiment sign-off
Data consent and regulatory exposure review
Guardrail configuration; no-peeking enforcement; auto-halt execution
Respond to auto-halt pages; root-cause harm events

A common failure mode is governance designed for enterprise compliance that effectively blocks the team from running experiments at all. Principles for keeping governance lean without removing its teeth:

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Risk-stratify aggressively.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' Most prompt experiments are low-risk and should auto-approve in under an hour. Gate depth should match actual risk, not regulatory theater.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': "Pre-register, don't retrofit.", 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' Subgroup analysis plans written after seeing the data are not governance — they are rationalization. The plan must be in the proposal.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Make the guardrail config part of the experiment definition,', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' not an afterthought. The experiment platform should refuse to launch without explicit thresholds.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Automate the audit trail.', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' Every gate decision, approval, and auto-halt is written to an append-only log automatically. Human reviewers should not be manually writing audit records.'}

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Treat a rejected experiment as learning,', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' not a failure. Teams that can revise and resubmit within a day maintain velocity. A rejection that takes two weeks to resolve will be gamed.'}

Governance is not the opposite of speed. Experiments blocked by poor governance — undiscovered subgroup harms, unplanned rollbacks, legal exposure — take far longer to resolve than experiments reviewed carefully up front.
Ethics review sign-off obtained in 10 minutes; conditions are never enforced
Ethics reviewer attends post-experiment review; conditions are pre-conditions to ship decision
Teams classify experiments as low-risk to avoid review delays
Platform classifies risk automatically from the proposal data, not self-reported
Guardrail thresholds set so high they never fire
Thresholds set from historical baselines + a safety margin; auto-halt is tested in staging before every launch
Subgroup breakdown produced only when primary metric is positive
Subgroups pre-registered; analysis runs unconditionally before ship decision

Experiment governance for GenAI is a four-gate pipeline: proposal risk classification (G1), ethics review covering harm, fairness, and privacy (G2), technical launch clearance (G3), and continuous runtime monitoring with automatic stop-loss (G4). Each gate has a defined owner, a documented decision, and an immutable audit record. Risk stratification keeps the pipeline fast for low-risk experiments without creating a blanket exception culture. The post-experiment review certifies the ship decision and closes the governance loop.
